translation: O you who have believed, do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies, extending to them affection while they have disbelieved in what came to you of the truth, having driven out the Prophet and yourselves [only] because you believe in Allah, your Lord. If you have come out for jihad in My cause and seeking means to My approval, [take them not as friends]. You confide to them affection, but I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared. And whoever does it among you has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ عَدُوِّى وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْحَقِّ يُخْرِجُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۙ أَن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ رَبِّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهَـٰدًا فِى سَبِيلِى وَٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِى ۚ تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَأَنَا۠ أَعْلَمُ بِمَآ أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَمَآ أَعْلَنتُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْهُ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

This surah is historically connected to the period close to the revelation of Surah At-Tawbah, around the time when discussions were taking place about the Muslims potentially marching on Makkah to take over the city. There was an internal conflict among the believers because many were concerned that if they invaded Makkah, there were still Muslims living there in secret—closet Muslims who had not yet migrated. If fighting broke out, it was feared that these hidden believers could become casualties in what would essentially be urban warfare, far more complicated than battlefield combat. The question arose: how would they distinguish between Muslims and non-Muslims during such chaos? This concern was addressed in Surah At-Tawbah.

Connected to this situation is the specific case of a companion named Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah (RA). He had family—a wife and children—still living in Makkah. Having escaped to Madinah with the Prophet ﷺ, he worried that when the conflict began, someone might retaliate against him by harming his family. So without informing the Prophet ﷺ, he wrote a letter to the Quraysh as a gesture of goodwill, essentially asking them to please spare his family if anything happened.

Though Hatib (RA) meant this as an innocent attempt to protect his loved ones, the letter would have given away the fact that the Muslims were making a move on Makkah—a critical military and strategic secret. Even though it was the season of Hajj, the Quraysh didn't know the Muslims' plan, and you don't want to give your enemy time to prepare. The Prophet ﷺ received revelation that this dangerous letter had actually left Madinah and was on its way to Makkah. He immediately dispatched Ali (RA) and another companion, telling them there was a woman at a specific location carrying a letter, and he wanted that letter returned immediately. None of this information came to the Prophet ﷺ except through revelation.

The companions found the woman exactly where they were told she would be. They searched her belongings but found nothing. They basically told her, "Our messenger told us you have a letter, and he doesn't lie. So unless you want us to search you completely, hand it over." She relented and pulled the letter from her hair, where she had hidden it in her bun. When the letter was brought back, it was discovered that Hatib (RA) had written it.

Umar (RA) wisely suggested severe punishment, but the Prophet ﷺ said no—Hatib was from the people of Badr, and their previous sins were forgiven. He let it slide. This is the subject matter being discussed at the beginning of Surah Al-Mumtahanah.

Related to this is another matter: the Treaty of Hudaybiyah had already been signed. According to its terms, if someone from the Muslims defected and became a disbeliever, running off to Makkah, he didn't have to be returned. But if someone escaped from Makkah and came to Madinah, he had to be returned. These subjects of exchange between Makkah and Madinah are dealt with in this surah.

The word المُمْتَحَنَة comes from the root meaning "to test" or "to examine"—specifically referring to a woman being tested. This connects to the testing of women who would escape from Makkah to determine the sincerity of their faith.

Allah ﷻ opens by saying, "Those of you who have iman, don't take My enemy and your enemy as protective friends." What were you thinking when you wrote that letter? They're not going to be your protective friends. They're not looking out for you. تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ—you're getting word out to them with love and compassion, trying to appeal to their sense of affection. But they've already disbelieved in whatever truth has come to you. 

يُخْرِجُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَإِيَّاكُمْ—they are the ones engaged in expelling the Messenger ﷺ. If they didn't show courtesy to the best of their men, the Prophet ﷺ himself, what makes you think they're going to show you courtesy? What were you thinking? They kicked you out too. And what was your crime? That you believed in Allah ﷻ, your Master.

إِن كُنتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهَـٰدًا فِى سَبِيلِى وَٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِى—if you came out to struggle in My path and pursue My pleasure, meaning if you did march forward, تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ—you're secretly getting information across to them with a feeling of mutually shared love and affection.

وَأَنَا۠ أَعْلَمُ بِمَآ أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَمَآ أَعْلَنتُمْ—and I know better what you have kept secret and what you've announced openly. وَمَن يَفْعَلْهُ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ—whoever among you does this has strayed far from the right course of action. He's been misled and lost from the even path, meaning he's way off from the correct course. He did something grossly wrong.

translation: If they gain dominance over you, they would be to you as enemies and extend against you their hands and their tongues with evil, and they wish you would disbelieve
commentary:
إِن يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا۟ لَكُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَيَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَأَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ

إِن يَثْقَفُوكُمْ—if they bumped into you, if they ran into you anywhere, if they found you by accident, يَكُونُوا۟ لَكُمْ أَعْدَآءً—they would have been enemies to you. وَيَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ—they would have extended their hands toward you, meaning they would have physically attacked you if they encountered you. وَأَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ—and they would have extended their tongues to you with evil intent, meaning they would have tried to confuse you about your religion or cursed you.

وَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ—and they have a loving hope, wishing that if only you would disbelieve. They want nothing more than for you to leave Islam.

translation: Never will your relatives or your children benefit you; the Day of Resurrection He will judge between you. And Allah, of what you do, is Seeing
commentary:
لَن تَنفَعَكُمْ أَرْحَامُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ ۚ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

لَن تَنفَعَكُمْ أَرْحَامُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ—your family ties and your children are not going to benefit you. يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ—on the Day of Resurrection, Allah ﷻ will cause judgment between all of you. وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ—and Allah ﷻ is in full view of whatever you're doing. He sees everything you do.

translation: There has already been for you an excellent pattern in Abraham and those with him, when they said to their people, "Indeed, we are disassociated from you and from whatever you worship other than Allah. We have denied you, and there has appeared between us and you animosity and hatred forever until you believe in Allah alone" except for the saying of Abraham to his father, "I will surely ask forgiveness for you, but I have not [power to do] for you anything against Allah. Our Lord, upon You we have relied, and to You we have returned, and to You is the destination
commentary:
قَدْ كَانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَءَٰٓؤُا۟ مِنكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةُ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءُ أَبَدًا حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَمَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ رَّبَّنَا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

قَدْ كَانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ—a good example, a beautiful role model for you to follow has already been laid down, especially in the case of Ibrahim (AS). وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ—and those who were with him. We're learning something rare in the Quran here: that Ibrahim (AS) had companions—sahaba—with him too, and they had a direct conflict with their society.

Historically, this surah is close to Surah At-Tawbah, also called Surah Al-Bara'ah—the surah of disassociation. We have nothing to do with that. This terminology of bara'ah (disassociation) is actually fulfilling the Sunnah of Ibrahim (AS), because his watchword and that of his followers, when they realized the disbelievers had reached the point of no return, was: إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَءَٰٓؤُا۟ مِنكُمْ—when they said to their nation, "We have nothing to do with any of you." وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ—"and we disassociate ourselves from all things that you worship besides Allah ﷻ."

كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ—"We no longer have faith in you people." وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةُ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءُ—"Between you and us, animosity has manifested, has come out into the open, and anger and hatred has come out between us." أَبَدًا—forever. حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥٓ—"until you come to believe in Allah ﷻ, the Only One."

إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ—the only exception to that being the statement of Ibrahim (AS) to his father. لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ—"I will absolutely ask Allah ﷻ to forgive you, Dad. I love you too much. I can't bear the thought of you burning in Hellfire, so I'm going to make dua to Allah ﷻ for you." As he's leaving, he says to his father, "I'll pray to my Master. He's been watching over me, and He'll take care of me." وَمَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ—"But I have no control. I can't control anything that happens to you from Allah ﷻ. I have no authority, no say in whatever comes to you from Allah ﷻ."

Then comes their beautiful dua: رَّبَّنَا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنَا—"Our Master, upon You alone we have placed our trust." وَإِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنَا—"And to You alone we return"—أَنَابَ means to return to someone in repentance, a spiritual return. وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—"And to You alone is the final place of return."

translation: Our Lord, make us not [objects of] torment for the disbelievers and forgive us, our Lord. Indeed, it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا رَبَّنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—"Our Master, do not turn us into a trial for those who disbelieve." Some scholars have interpreted this to mean: Allah ﷻ, don't make us the test subjects for the crimes of the disbelievers. The disbelievers are going to commit all kinds of atrocities—don't use us as victims of their atrocities. This interpretation is especially important for the companion who's concerned about his family: "Ya Allah, don't make my family victims of the crimes of the disbelievers."

But there's another deeply significant meaning. Ibn Kathir (may Allah have mercy on him) explains that لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ implies that our behavior, our course of action, our lack of character or lack of truthfulness could become proof for disbelievers to remain disbelievers. They won't be interested in Islam because of what they see in Muslims. "Allah ﷻ, don't make us a fitna because we will become the trial for the disbeliever." If something keeps you from guidance, attacks you and turns you off, it becomes a difficult trial for you—an obstacle in the path of truth. "Ya Allah, don't make us Muslims an obstacle to people coming to Islam."

SubhanAllah, we need this dua more than ever before today. رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—how many things are literally turn-offs from Muslims that people assume must be part of Islam? Things in our behavior, in our character, in the way we conduct ourselves individually and as a people that make others think, "Why would anybody want anything to do with Islam? Why would they want that?"

You can yell and scream that the system of government in the West is corrupt or unjust, that it's not the law of Allah ﷻ. But what have you done? You're one-fifth of the world's population. What justice do you have? Some of the most corrupt individuals hold positions of power in Muslim lands. Some of the most corrupt business practices exist there. Some of the most corrupt nations in the world by any index—Muslim, Muslim, Muslim. And we say, "Oh man, we lost the number eight spot to some polytheistic nation. At least we're in the top ten of some index, right? At least we're on top of something."

This is the reality. رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—Islam is something the entire world needs. But today the world says, "Why would I want Islam? Look at these people. You want us to be like that?" And I have nothing to say in response. What am I going to discuss? Theory? The life of Umar (RA)? "There was a lot of justice back then." That's beautiful. What's happening now?

I watched a debate—and I don't recommend watching it—between atheists who are against Islam. They were debating bodies of all things. It was like watching a frying pan and fire having a debate. But I was curious what the atheists had to say. Their arguments were that Islam oppresses women, this and that. The Muslim debater didn't say anything I or any other Muslim wouldn't have said. The atheist responded, "That's really nice. It's beautiful. But that's not what's happening. Here's what's happening. Here are the statistics. Here's what your courts do. Here's what your judges are doing. Here's what's happening with women here and here. This Sharia exists. You're talking about a Sharia in some book somewhere. I'm saying this Sharia that exists should not exist. That's all I'm saying." And I had nothing to say.

Because if the law of Islam is being used to perpetuate injustice and is being manipulated, we have to clean house before we can make any real dawah. At the individual level, dawah will go on. But at the institutional level, at the level of an ummah, we're not calling people to anything. We're not in a position to be writing letters like the Prophet ﷺ wrote to heads of state. He wrote those letters because he could show them a society based on Islam. "Come see what it looks like." What are we going to write? We have a lot of cleaning to do. There's a lot of laundry that needs to be done before we show people what this thing really is.

رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا رَبَّنَآ—"And forgive us, our Master." إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ—"Certainly You are Al-Aziz, the Ultimate Authority, Al-Hakim, the Wise."

Let me add one more thing to this. How much damage has been done to the dawah of Islam by the singular events of September 11th? How much? Every conversation with a non-Muslim about Islam somehow ends up in terrorism. The actions of a few have become a fitna for millions, if not billions, to never consider Islam. They won't consider it. "Those people are crazy."

You could say there are political reasons, economic reasons—yes, great. But they're not Islamic reasons. Yet the behavior of Muslims is automatically assumed to be Islam itself. There's no way around that, no escape. "No, no, some Muslims are that way, but that's not really Islam." We can have that conversation among ourselves, but there's no point having it with the outside world. All they know of Islam is what Muslims do. They're not interested in an Islam that comes from a book. They're interested in seeing what Islam does in life—how is it affecting the world?

That's why the irresponsibility of a few can destroy the work of dawah, can completely and utterly annihilate it. The practical manifestations of our deen, living up to the teachings of our deen—that's the real shahadah. That's the real witness against humanity.

translation: There has certainly been for you in them an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day. And whoever turns away - then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy
commentary:
لَقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ

لَقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ—certainly for you, in all of them—meaning Ibrahim (AS) and his followers who stood up to falsehood—there is a beautiful role model, an excellent example. لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ—for whoever would be hopeful of Allah ﷻ and the Last Day.

وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ—and whoever would turn back. فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ—then certainly Allah ﷻ, in fact, He is Al-Ghani and Al-Hamid.

Let's understand these beautiful names. الْغَنِىُّ means someone who doesn't need anybody else. It's an ism sifah mushabbahah—a constant, permanent attribute—which means He's never going to be in a position where He needs anybody else. Completely independent. That's ghina. When you make someone free of need, make someone independent, that's aghna. The word غِنَى means to be free of need.

Now the word الْحَمِيدُ. There are two words in Arabic: Hamid and Mahmud. مَحْمُود is an ism maf'ul, meaning someone whose praise has been done—it's done to them. It has the same pattern as مَضْرُوب (struck), مَأْكُول (eaten). So Mahmud means the one whose praise is done by someone.

But Hamid is also on the pattern of maf'ul—they say this pattern can carry the meaning of maf'ul—but the benefit of this particular word is profound. It means the one who is praised, but the one doing the praising isn't even mentioned. Whether someone is around or not, He still has praise. For Mahmud, you need someone there—there's someone missing linguistically who's doing the praising. But with Hamid, there is no one missing. He is praised in and of Himself, whether anybody else exists or not.

No other creation needs to exist for Allah ﷻ to be Al-Hamid. But something needs to exist for someone to be mahmud. So if you turn away from the religion, "I don't need you. And it's not like I need anybody else to praise Me either, because I am Al-Hamid." If the only time you're praised is when others praise you, then you're mahmud. No one but Allah ﷻ is truly Hamid. Someone could be mahmud, but nobody can be hamid except Allah ﷻ Himself.

translation: Perhaps Allah will put, between you and those to whom you have been enemies among them, affection. And Allah is competent, and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
۞ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَجْعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ عَادَيْتُم مِّنْهُم مَّوَدَّةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ قَدِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

"It may well be that Allah ﷻ will place affection between you and those you have enmity with among them. And Allah ﷻ is All-Powerful, and Allah ﷻ is Forgiving, Most Merciful."

Allah ﷻ opens this passage with remarkable hope. After commanding the believers to distance themselves from those who showed hostility to Islam, this ayah introduces a powerful possibility: that the very people who were once enemies might become allies through love. The word عَسَى indicates hope and possibility, reminding us that hearts can change in ways we cannot predict.

This transformation became a lived reality in Islamic history. When Islam achieved victory and Makkah was conquered, many who had fiercely opposed the Prophet ﷺ eventually embraced the faith. The Quran references this phenomenon in Surah An-Nasr: "When there comes the help of Allah ﷻ and the conquest, and you see people entering into the religion of Allah ﷻ in multitudes..." The majority of Makkans accepted Islam during the four-month grace period mentioned in Surah At-Tawbah. They were given time to reflect, to ask questions, to truly understand the message—not through force, but through patient invitation.

The wisdom behind this patient approach is profound. When someone comes seeking knowledge about Islam, we are instructed not to be harsh with them. The principle is simple: they have no knowledge yet, so give them time to learn and understand. This is the prophetic methodology—to create space for hearts to turn toward truth naturally.

The ayah concludes with two of Allah's beautiful names: وَٱللَّهُ قَدِيرٌ (Allah ﷻ is All-Powerful) and وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Allah ﷻ is Forgiving, Most Merciful). His power means He can transform the hardest of hearts, and His forgiveness and mercy extend even to those who once stood in opposition to His message.

translation: Allah does not forbid you from those who do not fight you because of religion and do not expel you from your homes - from being righteous toward them and acting justly toward them. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly
commentary:
لَّا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ

"Allah ﷻ does not forbid you from dealing kindly and justly with those who have not fought you because of your religion and have not driven you out of your homes. Indeed, Allah ﷻ loves those who are just."

This ayah establishes one of the most important principles in Islamic social relations: Muslims are not commanded to have animosity toward all non-Muslims. Rather, our position is specifically toward those who have shown hostility to us and our faith. Allah ﷻ explicitly states that He does not prevent believers from being good and fair to those who haven't fought against them in matters of religion and haven't expelled them from their homes.

The ayah uses two powerful words: تَبَرُّوهُمْ from birr, meaning goodness, kindness and righteousness, and تُقْسِطُوٓا۟ meaning to be just and fair. This isn't merely about tolerating non-Muslims—it's about actively showing them kindness and maintaining transparent, just relations with them. This represents the baseline of Muslim character: we engage with all people with excellence in conduct unless they have shown clear hostility toward Islam and Muslims.

The ayah concludes with a divine seal of approval: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (Indeed, Allah ﷻ loves those who are just). This isn't just permission—it's an invitation to earn Allah's love through fair treatment of all people. Our animosity is reserved for those who hate Allah's religion and fight against it. Everyone else deserves our good character and justice.

translation: Allah only forbids you from those who fight you because of religion and expel you from your homes and aid in your expulsion - [forbids] that you make allies of them. And whoever makes allies of them, then it is those who are the wrongdoers
commentary:
إِنَّمَا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ وَظَـٰهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْرَاجِكُمْ أَن تَوَلَّوْهُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

"Allah ﷻ only forbids you from those who fought you because of your religion, drove you out of your homes, and supported your expulsion—that you take them as allies. And whoever takes them as allies, it is they who are the wrongdoers."

Having established who Muslims should maintain good relations with, this ayah clarifies who we must not befriend. Allah ﷻ uses إِنَّمَا, a particle of restriction, to emphasize that the prohibition is specifically limited to a particular group: those who actively fought against Muslims because of their faith, expelled them from their homes, and supported others in driving them out.

The word ظَـٰهَرُوا۟ indicates those who made advances, proclaimed support for persecution, and actively assisted in the oppression of Muslims. These are not passive bystanders but active participants in hostility. For such people, Allah ﷻ forbids أَن تَوَلَّوْهُمْ —that you take them as close allies, friends or protectors, assuming they will come to your aid.

The ayah ends with a severe warning: وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (And whoever takes them as allies, it is they who are the wrongdoers). To befriend and seek protection from those who openly war against Islam is not just unwise—it constitutes dhulm, wrongdoing against oneself and the community.

translation: O you who have believed, when the believing women come to you as emigrants, examine them. Allah is most knowing as to their faith. And if you know them to be believers, then do not return them to the disbelievers; they are not lawful [wives] for them, nor are they lawful [husbands] for them. But give the disbelievers what they have spent. And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their due compensation. And hold not to marriage bonds with disbelieving women, but ask for what you have spent and let them ask for what they have spent. That is the judgement of Allah; He judges between you. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ مُهَـٰجِرَٰتٍ فَٱمْتَحِنُوهُنَّ ۖ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِهِنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ عَلِمْتُمُوهُنَّ مُؤْمِنَـٰتٍ فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ ۖ لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُّونَ لَهُنَّ ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مَّآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا۟ بِعِصَمِ ٱلْكَوَافِرِ وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقْتُمْ وَلْيَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

"O you who believe, when believing women come to you as emigrants, test them. Allah ﷻ knows best about their faith. But if you know them to be believers, do not return them to the disbelievers. They are not lawful for them, nor are they lawful for them. Give the disbelievers what they have spent. And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their dowries. And do not hold on to marriage bonds with disbelieving women. Ask for what you have spent, and let them ask for what they have spent. That is the judgment of Allah ﷻ; He judges between you. And Allah ﷻ is Knowing, Wise."

This ayah addresses a unique situation that arose during the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The treaty stipulated that any man who escaped from Makkah to Medina had to be returned. However, when Muslim women began fleeing persecution in Makkah to seek refuge in Medina, a complex predicament emerged. The Prophet ﷺ took the position that the agreement applied to men, not women, and these believing women would not be returned to an environment where their faith and safety were threatened.

However, this created a security concern. These women were coming from enemy territory during a state of war. The Muslim community didn't know which women were genuine believers and which might be spies sent to gather intelligence or create internal discord. This was a real threat that required a careful policy.

Allah ﷻ addresses this by commanding: فَٱمْتَحِنُوهُنَّ—test them, examine them, conduct a thorough questioning. This was essentially a border control policy rooted in divine guidance. The Arabic word used is not the stronger term مُؤْمِنَـٰت (believers) at first, but rather the general term indicating they claim belief, because only Allah ﷻ truly knows what's in their hearts.

The ayah immediately acknowledges this reality: ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِهِنَّ (Allah ﷻ knows best about their faith). You cannot see into hearts, but you can ask questions, observe consistency, and make reasonable judgments about sincerity. This is why the examination was necessary—not out of harshness, but out of legitimate security concerns during wartime.

Once satisfied that these women are indeed believers, the command is clear: فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ (do not return them to the disbelievers). These women may have had disbelieving husbands back in Makkah, but their migration for faith created an automatic severance of that marriage bond. The ayah states emphatically: لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُونَ لَهُنَّ (They are not lawful for them, nor are they lawful for them). The marriage is dissolved because the fundamental basis of a marital bond—shared values and faith—no longer exists.

What follows demonstrates the extraordinary justice of Islam, even toward enemies. وَءَاتُوهُم مَّآ أَنفَقُوا۟ (Give them what they have spent)—meaning whatever dowry or marriage gifts the disbelieving husbands had given should be refunded to them. SubhanAllah, even in a state of war, even when dealing with people ready to kill Muslims, the Quran commands financial fairness. These men lost their wives through no fault of their own from a transactional perspective, so they deserve their property back.

The ayah then addresses the Muslim community's concerns about these women: وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ (There is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their dowries). These women arrived without family, without guardians, in vulnerable circumstances. It would be easy for men to take advantage of their desperation and marry them without proper mahr or consideration. Allah ﷻ protects these women by emphasizing that they must be given proper dowries—no shortcuts, no exploitation of their situation.

The reciprocal ruling follows: وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا۟ بِعِصَمِ ٱلْكَوَافِرِ (Do not hold on to marriage bonds with disbelieving women). If Muslim men had wives back in Makkah who remained disbelievers, those marriages should be dissolved as well. The word عِصَمِ refers to the protective bond of marriage, and this bond cannot remain when faith divides a couple.

Then comes another command of fairness: وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقْتُمْ وَلْيَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ (Ask for what you have spent, and let them ask for what they have spent). Muslim men should request the return of whatever they spent on their now-divorced disbelieving wives, and the disbelievers have the right to request the same. Of course, whether the disbelieving women would actually return the gifts is another matter, but Islam establishes the right to ask.

The ayah concludes powerfully: ذَٰلِكُمْ حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ (That is the judgment of Allah ﷻ; He judges between you). This isn't merely a suggestion—it's divine legislation, a complete legal framework addressing immigration, security, marriage, divorce and financial rights. وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (And Allah ﷻ is Knowing, Wise)—He knows all circumstances and legislates with perfect wisdom.

translation: And if you have lost any of your wives to the disbelievers and you subsequently obtain [something], then give those whose wives have gone the equivalent of what they had spent. And fear Allah, in whom you are believers
commentary:
وَإِن فَاتَكُمْ شَىْءٌ مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ فَعَاقَبْتُمْ فَـَٔاتُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ذَهَبَتْ أَزْوَٰجُهُم مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ

"And if any of your wives escape to the disbelievers, and then you gain advantage, give those whose wives have gone the equivalent of what they had spent. And fear Allah ﷻ, in whom you are believers."

This ayah addresses a practical financial complication. What if a Muslim man's wife left him and returned to the disbelievers, and those disbelievers refused to refund the mahr he had paid? He would be in a difficult position—no wife, no money, unable to remarry because his resources are tied up in an unrecoverable loss.

Allah ﷻ provides a compassionate solution through the concept of communal support. فَعَاقَبْتُمْ means "when you gain advantage" or "when you acquire spoils of war in the next battle." When the Muslim community gains wealth through legitimate means in conflict with those same disbelievers, a portion should be set aside to compensate these men who lost both their wives and their wealth.

فَـَٔاتُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ذَهَبَتْ أَزْوَٰجُهُم مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ (Give those whose wives have gone the equivalent of what they spent). This became part of the distribution of spoils—using communal resources to help these men recover financially so they could move forward with their lives and potentially remarry.

The ayah concludes with a reminder of taqwa: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (And fear Allah ﷻ, in whom you are believers). This entire system of financial fairness, even with enemies, even in difficult circumstances, is rooted in consciousness of Allah ﷻ. The believers' faith demands they uphold justice and take care of each other, even when it's costly or complicated.

translation: O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging to you that they will not associate anything with Allah, nor will they steal, nor will they commit unlawful sexual intercourse, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their arms and legs, nor will they disobey you in what is right - then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ يُبَايِعْنَكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن لَّا يُشْرِكْنَ بِٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَسْرِقْنَ وَلَا يَزْنِينَ وَلَا يَقْتُلْنَ أَوْلَـٰدَهُنَّ وَلَا يَأْتِينَ بِبُهْتَـٰنٍ يَفْتَرِينَهُۥ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ وَلَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِى مَعْرُوفٍ ۙ فَبَايِعْهُنَّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُنَّ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

"O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging to you that they will not associate anything with Allah ﷻ, nor will they steal, nor will they commit adultery, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their hands and feet, nor will they disobey you in what is right—then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them from Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving, Most Merciful."

The Prophet ﷺ took several pledges of allegiance throughout his mission—at Aqabah, at Hudaybiyyah, and on other occasions. Many of these pledges are referenced in the Quran, but remarkably, only one is spelled out completely, term by term, condition by condition: the pledge taken from women. This is a unique honor that demonstrates the significance Islam places on women's active participation and commitment to the faith.

A pledge of allegiance serves multiple purposes. It creates a sense of belonging, establishes loyalty, and forms an emotional bond with the community and its mission. When people take citizenship oaths today, they often cry because they realize the weight of what they've committed to. For those born into a nation, the anthem might feel routine, but for someone newly joining, it's profoundly meaningful. Similarly, this bay'ah for women created a powerful sense of citizenship within Islam—a formal acknowledgment of their place in the Muslim community.

While men's pledges often involved military service and physical defense, women's pledge focused on spiritual and social commitments that would protect the fabric of society from within. The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to accept the pledge when women commit to specific principles:

أَن لَّا يُشْرِكْنَ بِٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا (That they will not associate anything with Allah ﷻ)—the foundation of faith, pure Tawhid, comes first.

وَلَا يَسْرِقْنَ (Nor will they steal)—respect for others' property and rights.

وَلَا يَزْنِينَ (Nor will they commit adultery)—protection of chastity, family bonds and social stability.

وَلَا يَقْتُلْنَ أَوْلَـٰدَهُنَّ (Nor will they kill their children)—a prohibition that addressed the practice of infanticide, particularly of girls, that existed in pre-Islamic Arabia, but also extends to protecting children from harm.

The next condition is particularly powerful: وَلَا يَأْتِينَ بِبُهْتَـٰنٍ يَفْتَرِينَهُۥ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ (Nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their hands and feet). The word بُهْتَـٰنٍ  refers to scandalous accusations and false rumors. The phrase "between their hands and feet" means wherever they go, whatever space they occupy, they will not spread destructive gossip.

This addresses one of the most damaging social behaviors—the casual spreading of rumors, the starting of scandals, the talking behind people's backs. In our age of social media, this has reached unprecedented levels. Research on teenagers and pre-teens shows that one of their greatest sources of anxiety is the fear of what people will say about them. A girl might not wear a sweater for a year because she's terrified of the comments she'll receive. Someone who refuses to smoke gets mocked both for not smoking and then falsely accused of smoking. The culture of casual slander destroys people's peace and confidence.

Allah ﷻ identifies this as a particularly female battleground—not because men don't engage in gossip, but because addressing it here emphasizes its importance in protecting social cohesion. When women commit to not spreading rumors and false accusations, they become guardians of society's moral fabric.

وَلَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِى مَعْرُوفٍ (Nor will they disobey you in what is right)—the word مَعْرُوفٍ  is significant. It means "that which is known and recognized." As the Quran was gradually revealed, what constituted ma'ruf evolved and expanded. In the early years of revelation, ma'ruf included basic values of justice, kindness and fairness. As more guidance was revealed and the Muslim community matured in their understanding, ma'ruf grew to encompass the complete framework of Islamic teachings.

By the time this ayah was revealed near the end of the Prophet's ﷺ life, most of the Shariah had been taught to Medinan society. Therefore, ma'ruf here represents the full corpus of Islamic values and commands that had become known and integrated into the community. This teaches us something powerful: you cannot command what hasn't been taught. Islam doesn't impose requirements on people who haven't been educated about them. The education of the religion is at the heart of reviving the religion, because as people's understanding grows, what they recognize as ma'ruf increases, and implementation becomes natural rather than forced.

After outlining these conditions, Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: فَبَايِعْهُنَّ (Then accept their pledge). This isn't just permission—it's an instruction to formalize their commitment and welcome them fully into the Muslim community.

وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُنَّ ٱللَّهَ (And ask forgiveness for them from Allah ﷻ)—immediately after accepting their pledge, the Prophet ﷺ is told to make dua for their forgiveness. This recognizes the reality that we all fall short and need Allah's mercy, even as we commit to these high standards. The Prophet's ﷺ supplication on their behalf is a source of barakah and divine support for their journey.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving, Most Merciful)—the ayah concludes with these two divine attributes, reminding us that no matter our past, Allah's forgiveness and mercy are always available when we turn to Him with sincerity.

translation: O you who have believed, do not make allies of a people with whom Allah has become angry. They have despaired of [reward in] the Hereafter just as the disbelievers have despaired of [meeting] the inhabitants of the graves
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ قَدْ يَئِسُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَمَا يَئِسَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْقُبُورِ

"O you who believe, do not take as allies a people with whom Allah ﷻ is angry. They have despaired of the Hereafter just as the disbelievers have despaired of those buried in graves."

The surah concludes by returning to its central theme: the believers' loyalty must be to Allah ﷻ and His cause, not to those who oppose it. On one side of the equation are those taking the pledge of allegiance, committing themselves to the values and community of Islam. On the other side are people that Allah ﷻ is angry with—those who have shown treachery and hostility toward the believers.

لَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ (Do not take as allies a people with whom Allah ﷻ is angry)—this is a clear prohibition against forming close bonds with those who have earned divine displeasure through their opposition to Islam and persecution of Muslims.

The ayah then describes their spiritual state: قَدْ يَئِسُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ (They have despaired of the Hereafter). The word يَئِسُوا۟ means complete hopelessness. These people have lost all hope regarding the afterlife, and this hopelessness manifests in their behavior and worldview.

The final phrase offers two possible interpretations, both profound: كَمَا يَئِسَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْقُبُورِ (Just as the disbelievers have despaired of those buried in graves).

The first interpretation: Just as disbelievers who have lost their loved ones have no hope of ever seeing them again, these people have lost hope in Allah ﷻ. If you want to witness true hopelessness, observe those who have lost family members but don't believe in resurrection or reunion. Their grief carries no light of future hope—it's absolute despair. These opponents of Islam carry that same quality of hopelessness.

The second interpretation: Just as the disbelievers who are already in their graves have lost all hope (because their opportunity for faith and good deeds has ended), these living opponents are similarly hopeless in their spiritual state. They're as good as dead in terms of their relationship with truth and hope. Their hearts are sealed, their chance at guidance seemingly gone.

The message is clear: don't befriend people characterized by such deep hopelessness and opposition to Allah ﷻ. Their despair will influence your own faith, their cynicism will erode your trust in Allah's promise, and their values will pull you away from the community of believers who live with hope in both this life and the next.

With this powerful conclusion, the surah completes its message about loyalty, boundaries, justice, and the protection of the Muslim community's faith and values. Alhamdulillah.
